input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building within the architecture, engineering, construction, and operations (AECO) domain, represented as a cyber-physical asset through integration of static models and runtime data for simulation, analysis, and optimization across its entire lifecycle from initial design to operational refurbishments and adaptations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real-time monitoring, predictive analytics for infrastructure performance, optimization of operational processes, and decision-making support for AECO stakeholders throughout the building lifecycle, including design, construction, and operation phases.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Modular architecture supports multiple DT instances with a centralized DT Framework acting as a hub; Building Control Engineers perform device modeling, Facility Managers handle real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers integrate services, each with distinct responsibilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points integrates custom services and analytics; the DT Framework serves as a central hub for bidirectional data exchange, coordinating roles such as Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers for holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes geometric BIM models enriched with semantic definitions of Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) to form a unified semantic foundation. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles transform these models into deployable DT artifacts, with real-time data exchange synchronizing physical and digital states. Custom analytical services and analytics are integrated via modular extension points for evolving operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modeled within BIM by Building Control Engineers as part of device configuration, with control logic defined for operational management. The framework uses Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware to enable real-time command execution and synchronization between the digital twin and physical actuators.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled directly within BIM environments with configurations specified as runtime semantics; data collection mechanisms integrate with the Building Control System (BCS) for real-time transmission of measurements.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to measure the accuracy of the digital twin's reflection of physical states, with verification processes ensuring alignment between modeled and real-world behaviors.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time sensor data exchange between physical assets and digital twin via Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware, utilizing runtime interfaces for continuous synchronization. Data transmission includes environmental and operational sensor readings with event-triggered updates.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for building infrastructure operations are transmitted from the digital twin to Building Control Systems (BCS) through runtime interfaces, with bidirectional communication ensuring command validation, execution confirmation, and feedback. Facility Managers directly operate physical infrastructure via the DT interface.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Technical connectivity employs a layered system architecture with Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware for IoT data ingestion and digital twin management, enabling protocol translation and data routing between BIM models and physical assets across heterogeneous systems.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, where sensors, actuators, and controllers are defined to address BIM's static limitations. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are then applied to extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Continuous real-time data exchange synchronizes the physical and digital twins, while interactive visualization enables monitoring and control. The modular architecture with standardized extension points supports integration of custom services and analytics, facilitating evolution to meet changing operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations, automatically generating deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points, enabling integration of custom services and analytics to dynamically respond to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The framework enables real-time monitoring, simulation, and predictive analytics for decision support, with interactive visualization interfaces for Facility Managers to interact with the digital twin. Custom services integrated via standardized extension points enhance insights generation, providing life cycle-encompassing optimization and data-driven operational decisions based on synchronized physical-digital data.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework addresses the absence of standardized integration frameworks by leveraging AECO-specific BIM standards for modeling and Eclipse Ditto for IoT communication standards (e.g., MQTT). It implements standardized extension points to ensure interoperability and modular service integration, while a modeling methodology standardizes BIM semantics to bridge static representations with dynamic DT requirements.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately synthesizes the definition of the physical system and its representation found in the text. It correctly identifies the system as a building in the AECO domain and captures the core concept of integrating static models with runtime data for specific purposes like simulation and optimization. Supported by quotes: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'along the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extracted description is highly specific and detailed, accurately synthesizing the service capabilities described in the abstract and introduction. It covers monitoring, predictive analytics, optimization, and decision-making support, explicitly linking them to the building lifecycle and stakeholders. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations', 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly names several specific tools and frameworks used to implement the Digital Twin, including BIM, Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine. Supported by quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', and 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the text's description of synchronization as 'real-time' and 'continuous', supported by the quote: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'. However, it misses the technical details of the implementation (Eclipse Hono and Ditto) mentioned in the contributions and abstract, preventing a higher score.

The extraction provides a comprehensive summary of the roles, architecture, and scope. It correctly identifies the DT Framework as a 'central hub', the architecture as 'modular', and accurately details the distinct responsibilities of the 'Building Control Engineers', 'Facility Managers', and 'DT Engineers' as listed in Table I and Section III-B: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub... coordinating roles such as Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers'.

This is a highly detailed and accurate synthesis of the system's orchestration. It correctly captures the 'modular architecture with standardized extension points', the 'central hub' role of the DT Framework, the 'bidirectional data exchange', and the coordination of roles for 'holistic building lifecycle management'. Quotes supporting this include: 'Modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics' and 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange'.

The extraction is a direct quote from the source and precisely identifies the external systems integrated. The Abstract explicitly states 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the source text. It correctly identifies the use of BIM models enriched with BCS components, the application of MDE principles for artifact generation, real-time synchronization, and modular extension points for services. Supported by quotes: 'enable building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a unified semantic foundation', 'framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations... and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts', and 'framework’s modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics'.

Correctly identifies that actuators are modeled in BIM by Building Control Engineers and that the Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware enables the real-time synchronization required for control. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... Models and configures devices, defines the control logic', 'defining... actuators... directly within the BIM environment', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The description is highly specific and accurately reflects the source's emphasis on embedding sensor configurations as runtime semantics within BIM and ensuring real-time data flow. The text explicitly states 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations... embedded into BIM models' and 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment'. It also supports the real-time aspect via 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The extraction accurately identifies the specific validation metric used in the study and its purpose. The source confirms that the authors 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and the goal of 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart'. The mention of verification processes is broadly supported by the context of synchronization and alignment discussed in the introduction.

It correctly specifies the use of runtime interfaces and continuous synchronization. The mention of environmental and operational readings is supported by the text's references to 'post-occupancy sensor data' and 'live operational data'. The phrase 'event-triggered updates' is a reasonable interpretation of 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization' supported by the text.

The description correctly captures the control flow from the digital twin to the Building Control Systems (BCS) and the role of runtime interfaces. It accurately identifies the Facility Manager as the actor who operates the infrastructure via the DT, as explicitly stated in the text. While 'command validation' and 'execution confirmation' are not explicitly detailed in the snippets, the general concept of 'bidirectional communication' and 'feed[ing] control signals back' is well supported.

The mention of 'heterogeneous systems' is strongly supported by the problem statement and solution proposal. While 'protocol translation' is not explicitly stated as a function in the text, the description aligns with the middleware's role in enabling 'interoperability' and 'bidirectional communication' between diverse systems.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document' regarding specific hosting infrastructure details such as cloud/edge/on-premise platforms, computational requirements, or storage specifications. While the text mentions software tools like 'Unreal Engine' and a 'model-driven framework', it does not define the underlying hosting deployment environment required by the characteristic description.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately synthesizes the lifecycle stages described in Section III.A and the Abstract. It correctly identifies the initial phase where Building Control Engineers model devices within BIM to define sensors and actuators, addressing BIM's static nature. It captures the subsequent application of Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to generate artifacts, the operational phase involving real-time data exchange via Eclipse Hono and Ditto, and the evolutionary potential through modular architecture. This aligns with the text: 'enables building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment' and 'modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics'.

The description correctly details the Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) approach for twinning, where enriched BIM models are transformed into deployable artifacts. It accurately reflects the framework's contribution to automation ('automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces') and the evolution management strategy ('standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'). The text supports this: 'Our approach systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and generates runtime interfaces' and 'standardized extension points for modular service integration, supporting scalable deployment and future extensibility'.

The extraction accurately captures the decision support capabilities, including real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and the user interaction role of the Facility Manager. It is strongly supported by the text. Quotes: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'Facility Manager... interacts with the twin', 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control'.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of BIM standards, the role of extension points for interoperability, and the methodology to standardize semantics. However, it includes an unsupported detail regarding 'MQTT' which is not present in the source text, slightly reducing the score. Quotes: 'absence of standardized integration frameworks', 'A modeling methodology to standardize BIM semantics', 'standardized extension points to ensure interoperability'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
